title: Indian Young Lawyers Association v. State of Kerala (Sabarimala Case)
legal_principles: Exclusion based on physiological characteristics is unconstitutional
relevant_acts: Kerala Hindu Places of Public Worship (Authorisation of Entry) Act 1965
summary: The Supreme Court (4:1) struck down the practice of excluding women aged 10-50 from entering the Sabarimala temple. The majority held this practice violated Articles 14, 15, and 17 (untouchability), and that 'constitutional morality' must prevail over popular religious morality.
key_holdings: Exclusion of women from temple based on biological/physiological characteristics is unconstitutional
Constitutional morality must prevail over popular morality
Right to worship is guaranteed to all, regardless of gender
Exclusionary religious practices that violate fundamental rights are not protected under Article 26
Dissent (Justice Indu Malhotra): Courts should not interfere in matters of religion
key_quotes: Devotion cannot be subjected to notions of purity and pollution based on physiological phenomena.
Constitutional morality is not natural to a society; it has to be cultivated.
why_important: Landmark judgment on intersection of religious freedom and gender equality. Generated significant social debate. Review petitions referred to 9-judge bench.
relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Demonstrates how constitutional rights interact with institutional practices — relevant to compliance frameworks.
